summary:
The deliberate cultivation of wheat stands as one of the most transformative developments in human prehistory. Einkorn wheat (Triticum monococcum) and emmer wheat (Triticum dicoccum) were domesticated from wild progenitors in the Fertile Crescent — particularly in the region of southeastern Anatolia and northern Syria — around 9500–8500 BCE. Genetic and archaeobotanical evidence places the origin zone near the Karacadag mountain range in modern Turkey, where wild einkorn populations still grow today.

The transition from harvesting wild grasses to cultivating domesticated varieties was gradual, likely spanning centuries. Early farmers selected plants with non-shattering seed heads — a key domestication trait that prevented the grain from dispersing naturally before harvest — dramatically increasing yields and storage potential. This morphological change, visible in the archaeological record, marks the beginning of true agriculture.

Wheat cultivation spread rapidly from its origins across the Fertile Crescent and into Anatolia, the Levant, and eventually Europe and South Asia. It became the dietary foundation of the world's earliest civilizations and continues to be one of the most important cereal crops globally. The shift to wheat farming enabled sedentary village life, population growth, and the accumulation of food surpluses that underpinned the emergence of social complexity.
